modules.title: Introduction to Figma & UI/UX Fundamentals
modules.content:
## Understanding the Digital Canvas: UI vs. UX

Imagine you are booking a flight for a long-awaited vacation. You open an app, and within three taps, you have selected your dates, chosen your seat, and completed the purchase. The process feels effortless, almost enjoyable. Now imagine a different scenario: you open the app, the buttons are too small to tap, the colors make the text impossible to read, and you have to start over twice because the app keeps crashing. You finally give up, close the app, and use a competitor's website instead.

Why did the first experience feel so smooth, and the second so frustrating? The answer lies in the combined power of UI and UX. 

When you start learning digital design, these two acronyms are thrown around constantly. While they are deeply interconnected, they refer to two distinct parts of the design process. Understanding the difference is the foundation of everything you will build in Figma.

### What is UX (User Experience)?

**UX**, or **User Experience**, is the process of designing a product that is useful, easy to use, and delightful to interact with. It is primarily about *how it works* and *how it feels*. 

UX design focuses on the logic and structure behind a product. Before a single color is chosen or a button is shaped, a UX designer is asking questions like:
* What is the user trying to accomplish?
* How many steps does it take them to reach their goal?
* Is the flow of information logical?
* What happens if the user makes a mistake? How do we help them recover?

UX is heavily research-based. It involves interviewing users, mapping out user journeys, and creating wireframes (basic, low-detail blueprints of a page). If a building were a digital product, UX would be the architectural blueprint, plumbing, and electrical wiring. You don't necessarily see them, but if they are flawed, the building is unlivable.

### What is UI (User Interface)?

**UI**, or **User Interface**, is the graphical layout of a product. It is about *how it looks*. 

UI design encompasses all the visual elements a user interacts with on a screen. This includes:
* **Typography:** The fonts used and how they are sized.
* **Color palettes:** The combination of colors that convey brand identity and hierarchy.
* **Buttons and icons:** The visual shapes users click or tap.
* **Spacing:** The distance between elements to ensure the design doesn't feel cluttered.

If UX is the architectural blueprint of a building, UI is the interior design—the paint on the walls, the style of the doorknobs, and the lighting fixtures. A UI designer takes the structural logic created by the UX designer and makes it visually appealing and accessible.

### The UI/UX Relationship

While UX is about logic and problem-solving, and UI is about visuals and aesthetics, they are deeply symbiotic. A beautiful interface (good UI) cannot fix a confusing, broken process (bad UX). Similarly, a brilliantly logical process (good UX) will still frustrate users if the text is unreadable or the buttons are invisible (bad UI).

Throughout this book, you will learn how to use Figma to address both. You will start by learning the tools to create visual interfaces (UI), and eventually use those tools to build logical, interactive experiences (UX).

## First Principles of Design

Before opening Figma, it helps to understand a few foundational concepts that guide how digital screens are built. 

### Pixels and Screen Sizes

A **pixel** is the smallest unit of color on a digital screen. When you design in Figma, you are essentially arranging colored pixels on a grid. However, not all screens have the same number of pixels. 

A smartphone screen is much smaller than a laptop monitor. As a designer, you cannot design a single, static image and expect it to work everywhere. You must design for different screen sizes. To do this, designers use specific **Artboards** (in Figma, we call these **Frames**) to represent common device dimensions. For example, a standard mobile frame might be 375 pixels wide, while a standard desktop frame might be 1440 pixels wide.

### Visual Hierarchy

**Visual hierarchy** is the principle of arranging elements to show their order of importance. It guides the user’s eye to the most important information first. 

Imagine a weather app. The current temperature is the most important piece of information, so it should be the largest text on the screen. The hourly forecast is secondary, so it is smaller. The settings icon is tertiary, so it is even smaller and tucked into a corner. You establish visual hierarchy through differences in size, color, contrast, and spacing.

### Accessibility

**Accessibility** in design means creating products that can be used by as many people as possible, including those with visual, motor, or cognitive disabilities. A common example is color contrast: if you put light gray text on a white background, people with low vision will struggle to read it. Good design isn't just about looking pretty; it is about ensuring everyone can use the product effectively.

## Getting Started with Figma

Now that you understand the landscape of digital design, it is time to learn the tool you will use to build it: Figma.

### What is Figma?

**Figma** is a cloud-based interface design tool. In the past, designers used software that had to be installed locally on their computers, making collaboration difficult. If a designer wanted to share a file, they had to email a massive attachment or use a file-sharing service. If two designers tried to work on the same file, they would overwrite each other's work.

Figma changed this by operating entirely in the web browser. Because it is cloud-based:
* **It is real-time:** Multiple people can design in the same file simultaneously, much like multiple people can edit a Google Doc at the same time.
* **It is cross-platform:** It works on Mac, Windows, and Linux, because it runs in a browser.
* **It is accessible:** You can share a link to your design with a developer or a client, and they can view it, leave comments, or inspect the code without downloading any software.

### Creating Your Account

To get started, open your web browser and navigate to figma.com. Click "Sign up" and create an account using your email address, or sign in with your Google account. 

While Figma runs in the browser, Figma also offers a dedicated desktop application for Mac and Windows. The desktop app performs slightly better than the browser version because it doesn't have to share resources with your browser tabs. For this chapter, either the browser or desktop app will work perfectly.

Once you log in, you will land on the Figma File Browser.

## Navigating the Figma Interface

The Figma workspace can look intimidating at first glance, but it is logically divided into distinct areas. Let’s break down the interface into its core components.

### The File Browser

When you first log into Figma, you are greeted by the **File Browser**. This is your home base. It displays all of the design files and projects you have created or been invited to. 

To get hands-on, let's create your first file:
1. In the top-left corner, click the **Drafts** folder. This is your personal, private workspace.
2. Click the **New file** button (often a plus icon or a blank rectangle) in the top toolbar.
3. A blank canvas will appear. You are now inside the Figma design environment.

### The Canvas

The **Canvas** is the infinite, gray, dotted background in the center of your screen. Unlike a physical piece of paper or a Photoshop document, the Figma canvas does not have edges. You can scroll and pan infinitely in any direction. 

This allows you to keep multiple screens, ideas, and versions of a design all in the same view. If you are designing a mobile app, you might put the login screen on the left, the home screen in the middle, and the settings screen on the right.

### The Toolbar

At the top of the screen sits the **Toolbar**. This is your primary toolbox. The tools available here change slightly depending on what you have selected on the canvas, but the core tools remain constant. 

Here are the most important tools for a beginner:

* **Move Tool (V):** The arrow icon. This is your default tool. Use it to select, drag, and reposition elements on the canvas.
* **Frame Tool (F):** The icon that looks like a rectangle with a dashed border. **Frames** are the foundational containers for your designs. You will use frames to define the boundaries of a mobile screen, a desktop webpage, or a specific UI component. 
* **Shape Tools (R, O, L):** This dropdown menu allows you to draw basic geometry. 
    * **R** creates a Rectangle.
    * **O** creates an Ellipse (circle).
    * **L** creates a Line.
* **Pen Tool (P):** Allows you to draw custom, freeform vector paths. (We won't use this much as beginners).
* **Text Tool (T):** The "T" icon allows you to click anywhere on the canvas and start typing.
* **Hand Tool (H):** The hand icon allows you to click and drag to pan around the canvas. 

**Pro tip:** You do not need to click the icons in the toolbar every time. Figma relies heavily on **keyboard shortcuts**. The letter in the parentheses above (V, F, R, T) is the shortcut key for that tool. Pressing the letter on your keyboard instantly activates the tool.

### The Left Sidebar: Layers, Assets, and Pages

The panel on the left side of your screen is your organizational hub. It has three main tabs at the top: **Layers**, **Assets**, and **Pages**.

* **Layers Panel:** As you add elements to your canvas—frames, rectangles, text, images—they appear as individual **layers** in this list. Much like stacking sheets of transparent plastic, layers at the top of the list sit visually *in front of* layers at the bottom of the list. Figma nests layers hierarchically. If you put a rectangle inside a frame, the frame is the "parent" and the rectangle is the "child." You can click the small dropdown arrow next to a parent layer to reveal its children.
* **Assets Panel:** This is where you will eventually store reusable design elements (called Components). We will cover this in a later chapter.
* **Pages Panel:** A single Figma file can hold multiple **Pages**. Think of pages like tabs in a spreadsheet. You might use Page 1 for your actual UI design, Page 2 for wireframing, and Page 3 for collecting inspiration. 

### The Right Sidebar: Design, Prototype, and Inspect

The panel on the right side of your screen is the **Properties Panel**. This is where the magic happens. When you select an object on your canvas, this panel populates with the specific properties of that object, allowing you to modify it with mathematical precision rather than just dragging it visually.

It has three main tabs:

* **Design (Shift + 1):** This is the tab you will use 90% of the time. It controls the visual appearance of an element. 
    * **Alignment:** Buttons to align objects left, right, center, top, or bottom relative to one another.
    * **Position (X and Y):** The exact horizontal (X) and vertical (Y) coordinates of the object on the canvas.
    * **Dimensions (W and H):** The exact Width and Height of the object in pixels.
    * **Layout:** Settings for organizing elements inside a frame (covered in a later chapter).
    * **Fill:** The interior color of the object.
    * **Stroke:** The border or outline color and thickness of the object.
    * **Effects:** Drop shadows, blurs, and other visual effects.
* **Prototype (Shift + 2):** This tab allows you to connect your screens and make them interactive, simulating how a user will click through the app. (Covered in a later chapter).
* **Inspect (Shift + 3):** This tab is primarily for developers. When you are ready to hand your design off to a programmer to build, they will use this tab to view the underlying CSS, iOS, or Android code snippets needed to recreate your design in a real programming environment.

## Your First Project Setup

Let’s put this foundational knowledge to work by setting up your first project page. A well-organized file is a professional file. When you open a blank Figma file, you are placed in a default page called "Page 1". 

### Setting Up Your Pages

Good organization starts at the page level. Let's rename our pages to reflect a professional workflow.

1. In the left sidebar, ensure you are on the **Pages** tab.
2. Double-click the text "Page 1". It will become editable.
3. Type "Cover & Notes" and press Enter. This page will be used to title your project and leave notes for your team.
4. Click the **+** icon next to the Pages header to create a new page.
5. Double-click the new page, rename it "Wireframes", and press Enter.
6. Create one more page by clicking the **+** icon. Double-click it, rename it "UI Design", and press Enter.

You now have a structured file. You will do your rough brainstorming on the Wireframes page, and your polished, final visual design on the UI Design page.

### Creating Your First Frame

Let's set up your first canvas space on the UI Design page.

1. Click on your newly created **UI Design** page in the left sidebar. The canvas should be completely empty.
2. Go to the top toolbar and select the **Frame Tool** (or press **F** on your keyboard). Notice that your cursor changes into a crosshair.
3. Look at the right sidebar. It has now updated to show a list of **Device Presets**. You will see categories for Phones, Tablets, Desktops, and Paper.
4. Under the "Phone" category, click **iPhone 14 / 13 Pro Max**. 

Instantly, a frame the exact size of an iPhone screen appears on your canvas. This is the boundary of your design. Anything you place inside this frame represents what will be visible on a user's phone screen. 

Notice the left sidebar. A layer named "iPhone 14 / 13 Pro Max" has appeared. 

### Adjusting the Frame

Even though you used a preset, Figma gives you total control over the dimensions. 

1. Select the **Move Tool** (press **V**) and click on your new frame on the canvas. A blue outline will appear around it, indicating it is selected.
2. Look at the right sidebar, specifically the **W** (Width) and **H** (Height) inputs. Because you selected an iPhone preset, these will be populated with the exact pixel dimensions of that device (e.g., 430 x 932). 
3. You can manually change these numbers. Click inside the W box, type `375`, and press Enter. Click inside the H box, type `812`, and press Enter. 

You have just resized your frame to the dimensions of a standard iPhone X/11/12. By defining the exact width and height, you ensure you are designing within the physical constraints of a real device.

### Panning and Zooming

Before we wrap up this setup, you need to know how to move around your infinite canvas. 

* **Zoom:** To zoom in and out, use your mouse wheel to scroll. If you are on a trackpad, pinch in and out with two fingers. You can also use the keyboard shortcuts: **Command (⌘) + Plus (+)** to zoom in, and **Command (⌘) + Minus (-)** to zoom out on a Mac. On Windows, use **Control (Ctrl) + Plus** and **Control (Ctrl) + Minus**.
* **Pan:** To move around the canvas without accidentally dragging your design elements, hold down the **Spacebar**. Your cursor will temporarily turn into a hand. Click and drag to pan across the canvas. Alternatively, use the **Hand Tool (H)**.
* **Zoom to Fit:** If you ever lose your frame on the infinite canvas, press **Shift + 1**. Figma will automatically zoom out and center your frame in the middle of your screen. This is a lifesaver for beginners.

You have now successfully defined the difference between UI and UX, navigated the core areas of the Figma interface, and set up a structured, properly sized project page. You have built the foundation. In the next chapter, we will start filling this empty frame using shapes, text, and layout grids.

## Key Takeaways

* **UX (User Experience)** focuses on how a product works and feels, relying on logic, structure, and user psychology.
* **UI (User Interface)** focuses on how a product looks, utilizing typography, color, and visual hierarchy.
* Good design requires both: a beautiful UI cannot fix a broken UX, and a logical UX is useless if the UI is unreadable.
* **Visual hierarchy** guides the user’s eye to the most important information first through variations in size, color, and spacing.
* **Figma** is a cloud-based, real-time collaborative design tool that runs in a web browser or desktop app.
* The Figma workspace consists of the infinite **Canvas**, the top **Toolbar**, the left **Layers/Pages/Assets sidebar**, and the right **Design/Prototype/Inspect Properties panel**.
* **Frames (F)** are the foundational containers for your designs, used to define the boundaries of specific device screens.
* **Keyboard shortcuts** (like V for Move, F for Frame, T for Text) are essential for working efficiently in Figma.
* Proper file organization starts immediately by renaming **Pages** to reflect your workflow (e.g., Wireframes, UI Design).
* Use **Spacebar + Drag** to pan, and **Shift + 1** to zoom to fit your design on the screen.

modules.title: Frames, Shapes, and Layout Grids
modules.content:
## Building the Blueprint: Understanding Frames

Imagine trying to paint a portrait without a canvas, or build a house without a foundation. In digital design, you cannot simply place elements into the empty void of the Figma Canvas. You need a defined boundary to hold your design, dictate its proportions, and establish how it responds to different screen sizes. In Figma, this foundational boundary is called a Frame.

As you may recall from our introduction, Figma uses Frames rather than traditional Artboards. While an artboard is essentially a static flat surface, a Frame is a dynamic, flexible container. It acts as a parent element that can dictate the layout rules for the child elements placed inside it. 

To create a Frame, select the **Frame Tool (F)** from the Toolbar and click anywhere on the Canvas. Alternatively, you can press the **F** key on your keyboard. Once the tool is active, you have two options:
1. **Click and drag** to draw a custom-sized Frame.
2. **Simply click** on the Canvas to drop a default Frame (usually 100x100 pixels). You can then resize it manually.

However, UI/UX designers rarely design for arbitrary, custom sizes. We design for specific devices like smartphones, tablets, and desktop monitors. When you select the Frame Tool, look at the right side of your screen. The Design panel updates to show a section called **Frame**. Here, you will see a list of preset device sizes organized by categories like Phone, Tablet, and Desktop.

If you click "iPhone 14" under the Phone presets, Figma instantly creates a Frame with the exact pixel dimensions of that device's screen (390 x 844 pixels). This ensures your designs are grounded in reality from the very first click.

### Anatomy of a Frame

Once a Frame is on your Canvas, it’s important to understand its anatomy. A Frame has a name (visible in the Layers panel on the left), a specific width and height (measured in pixels), and a set of properties that define its background and boundaries.

When you select a Frame, the Design panel on the right displays several key sections:
*   **Layout**: Shows the width (W) and height (H) of the Frame. You can type exact numbers here to resize it precisely.
*   **Fill**: Unlike a plain canvas, a Frame comes with a default background color. You can change this color, add gradients, or even apply images as a background.
*   **Stroke**: You can add a border (stroke) to the outside of your Frame, which is helpful when designing multiple frames side-by-side and you need to see exactly where one screen ends and another begins.
*   **Clip Content**: This is a vital checkbox in the properties panel. When checked, any shape or element you place inside the Frame that spills over the edges will be visually "clipped" (hidden) so it doesn't stick out. This mimics how a real phone screen hides anything that goes beyond its physical glass edge.

### Nesting Frames

One of the most powerful features of Frames is that they can be placed inside other Frames. This concept, called **nesting**, is the secret to organizing complex user interfaces.

For example, you might have a main "Phone Screen" Frame. Inside that Frame, you might want a navigation bar at the bottom. Instead of drawing four separate buttons floating loosely on the screen, you can draw a smaller Frame inside the main Frame to hold the navigation bar. 

The main Frame is the parent, and the navigation bar Frame is the child. By organizing your designs this way, you create a logical structure in your Layers panel that makes your file easy to navigate, update, and hand off to developers later.

## Drawing and Manipulating Shapes

While Frames provide the container, **Shapes** provide the actual visual content of your interface. Buttons, input fields, profile pictures, and icons are all built using fundamental shapes. 

You can access the **Shape Tools (R, O, L)** from the Toolbar. The dropdown reveals several options, but the three most essential for UI design are the Rectangle, Ellipse, and Line.

### The Rectangle Tool (R)

The Rectangle is the most heavily used shape in UI design. It forms the basis of cards, buttons, text fields, and image containers. 

1. Select the **Rectangle Tool (R)** or press **R**.
2. Click and drag anywhere on the Canvas to draw the shape.
3. **Holding Shift** while dragging locks the aspect ratio, creating a perfect square.
4. **Holding Option (Mac) or Alt (Windows)** while dragging draws the rectangle from the center outward, rather than from the top-left corner.

Once a rectangle is drawn, you can manipulate it using the **Move Tool (V)**. Select the rectangle, and you will see bounding box handles on all four sides and corners. Clicking and dragging these handles allows you to resize the shape. 

In the Design panel, a selected rectangle reveals new properties:
*   **Corner Radius**: By default, rectangles have sharp, 90-degree corners. By increasing the corner radius value (the small arc icon next to the dimensions), you can round the corners. This is a staple of modern UI design, softening the harshness of strict grids. You can even click the "Independent corners" icon to round only specific corners, which is useful for creating shapes like tabs or speech bubbles.
*   **Fill and Stroke**: Just like Frames, shapes can have their own background colors (Fill) and borders (Stroke).

### The Ellipse Tool (O)

The Ellipse Tool is used to create ovals and circles. Circles are incredibly common in UI design, used for profile avatars, notification badges, radio buttons, and loading spinners.

1. Select the **Ellipse Tool (O)** or press **O**.
2. Click and drag to draw an ellipse.
3. **Holding Shift** while dragging will constrain the shape to a perfect circle.

If you need a specific size—for example, a 48x48 pixel profile picture—you can draw any random circle, select it, and type `48` into both the W and H fields in the Design panel. Figma will instantly snap the ellipse to those exact dimensions.

### The Line Tool (L)

Lines are used to create dividers between sections of content, underline text, or construct simple icons. 

1. Select the **Line Tool (L)** or press **L**.
2. Click and drag to draw a line. 
3. **Holding Shift** while dragging constrains the line to perfectly horizontal, vertical, or 45-degree angles.

Unlike rectangles and ellipses, lines do not have a Fill property because they are essentially a path with no enclosed area. Instead, they are styled entirely by their **Stroke**. You can adjust the stroke's color, and crucially, its thickness (measured in pixels). A 1px line is standard for subtle dividers, while a 3px or 4px line might be used for a bold underline.

### A Realistic Scenario: Building a Profile Card

Let’s put these tools together in a practical scenario. Imagine you need to design a user profile card for a mobile app.

1. Draw a **Frame** using the iPhone preset to establish your screen boundary.
2. Select the **Rectangle Tool (R)** and draw a card shape inside the Frame, leaving some padding around the edges.
3. Give the rectangle a slight corner radius (e.g., 12px) to soften its appearance.
4. Select the **Ellipse Tool (O)**, hold **Shift**, and draw a circle near the top of the rectangle to serve as the profile picture placeholder.
5. Select the **Line Tool (L)**, hold **Shift**, and draw a horizontal line near the bottom of the rectangle to separate the user's name from their bio.

In just a few clicks, you have moved from a blank canvas to a structured, recognizable piece of a user interface.

## Applying Layout Grids and Guides

Designing without structure often leads to misaligned elements, inconsistent spacing, and a final product that feels chaotic. To achieve **Visual hierarchy** and maintain a clean layout, designers use underlying structures to guide their placement of shapes and text. In Figma, these structures come in the form of Layout Grids and Ruler Guides.

### Layout Grids

A Layout Grid is a non-printing, non-exporting visual overlay that sits on top of your Frame to help you align elements. It does not show up when you export your design or present a prototype; it is strictly a tool for the designer.

To add a Layout Grid, select a Frame (or any shape), scroll down the Design panel to the **Layout Grid** section, and click the **+** icon. By default, Figma applies a "Grid" (a series of uniform squares). However, clicking the text that says "Grid" opens a dropdown menu with three distinct types of grids:

1.  **Grid**: A pattern of evenly spaced horizontal and vertical lines (or just dots). This is best for icon design or aligning elements that need to snap to a strict, pixel-perfect baseline.
2.  **Columns**: This is the most commonly used grid in UI design. It divides your Frame into a set number of vertical columns. You can define the number of columns (e.g., 4 for mobile, 12 for desktop), the width of the columns, and the "Gutter" (the empty space between each column). This mimics the CSS grid systems used by web developers, ensuring your designs translate perfectly to code.
3.  **Rows**: Similar to columns, but horizontal. This is useful for establishing vertical rhythm, ensuring elements like buttons and text fields align consistently from top to bottom.

You can adjust the color and opacity of your grid. Most designers lower the opacity to around 10% or 20% so the grid is visible but doesn't distract from the actual design elements.

### Ruler Guides

While Layout Grids are systematic and predefined, sometimes you just need a quick, manual reference line to align two specific elements. This is where Ruler Guides come in.

To use guides, you first need to enable your rulers. Go to the top menu and select **View > Rulers** (or use the shortcut Shift + R). A ruler will appear along the top and left edges of your Figma window.

To create a guide, simply click inside the top ruler and drag your mouse downward. A blue horizontal line will appear on your Canvas. Click inside the left ruler and drag right to create a vertical blue line.

You can drag these guides anywhere on your Canvas. As you move shapes around, Figma will snap the edges and centers of your shapes to these guides, making manual alignment effortless. To remove a guide, simply click the blue line and drag it all the way back into the ruler, or double-click the guide to delete it instantly.

### The Importance of Spacing

Both grids and guides are ultimately tools to help you manage **Spacing**. Consistent spacing is what separates an amateur design from a professional one. By using a column grid, you ensure that your shapes always start and end at predictable intervals. By using guides, you can ensure that the top margin of your header matches the side margins of your content area. 

## Structuring Your First Screen

Now that you understand how to create containers, draw shapes, and apply structural overlays, let's look at how these elements work together to form the foundation of a UI screen.

When you start a new design, the workflow generally follows these steps:

1.  **Define the Canvas**: Press **F** and select your device preset from the right panel. This establishes your physical boundaries and screen aspect ratio.
2.  **Establish the Structure**: With the Frame selected, add a **Columns** Layout Grid. For a mobile screen, 4 or 6 columns is standard. This gives you an invisible skeleton for your layout.
3.  **Block Out the Content**: Use the **Rectangle** and **Ellipse** tools to create "placeholders" for your content. Don't worry about colors or typography yet (we will cover those in the next chapter). Focus purely on size and position. Draw a rectangle where your header will go, an ellipse where your image will sit, and smaller rectangles where your buttons will be.
4.  **Align and Adjust**: Use your Move Tool (V) to drag these shapes into position, letting them snap to your Layout Grid. Use the bounding box handles to resize them until the **Spacing** feels balanced and the **Visual hierarchy** is clear (e.g., making the "Sign Up" button rectangle larger than the "Forgot Password" text area).

By following this process, you build a wireframe—a structural blueprint of your interface. You are defining the skeleton before adding the skin. This ensures that your layout is solid, your spacing is consistent, and your design is accessible before you ever spend time picking the perfect colors or fonts.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Frames are dynamic containers**: Unlike flat Artboards, Frames are flexible parent containers that form the foundation of any screen. Use the **Frame Tool (F)** and device presets to ensure your designs match real-world screen dimensions.
*   **Nesting organizes design**: Placing Frames inside other Frames creates a logical parent-child relationship in your Layers panel, keeping complex designs manageable.
*   **Shapes build the visual UI**: Use the **Rectangle (R)**, **Ellipse (O)**, and **Line (L)** tools to construct buttons, cards, images, and dividers. Remember to hold **Shift** to constrain proportions and **Alt/Option** to draw from the center.
*   **Properties control appearance**: The Design panel allows you to precisely control dimensions (W/H), Corner Radii, Fills, and Strokes without having to redraw shapes.
*   **Layout Grids provide invisible structure**: Applying Column or Grid overlays to your Frames ensures consistent alignment and spacing. They are visible only to the designer and do not export with the final design.
*   **Ruler Guides offer manual alignment**: Use Shift + R to toggle rulers, and drag lines onto your Canvas to quickly snap and align specific elements.
*   **Structure before style**: A professional workflow involves defining the Frame, setting the grid, and blocking out shapes to establish spacing and hierarchy before moving on to detailed styling.

modules.title: Typography and Color Styling
modules.content:
## The Invisible Architecture of Reading

Imagine walking into a grocery store where every item—from the aisle signs to the nutrition labels on a cereal box—is written in the exact same font size and weight. Finding a simple carton of milk would become a frustrating, exhausting scavenger hunt. 

Digital interfaces suffer the same fate without clear **visual hierarchy**. As we discussed in earlier chapters, visual hierarchy guides the user’s eye to the most important information first. Two of the most powerful tools you have to build this hierarchy are typography and color. 

When you open a new file in Figma and drop a Frame onto your Canvas, it starts as a blank shell. By adding text layers, applying specific fonts, and filling shapes with color, you transform that empty Frame into a recognizable User Interface. This chapter will teach you how to manipulate these properties to create designs that are not only aesthetically pleasing but also highly readable and accessible to your users.

## Working with Text Layers

In Figma, any piece of text—whether it’s a single character or a multi-paragraph essay—lives inside a **Text Layer**. 

To add a Text Layer to your design, select the Text Tool from the Toolbar (or press the **T** key). Your cursor will turn into a crosshair. Click anywhere on your Canvas or inside an existing Frame, and you can start typing. Once you are done, press the **Escape** key or select the Move Tool (**V**) to exit text editing mode.

### Understanding Typography Properties

When you select a Text Layer, the Right Sidebar populates with a variety of typography properties. For beginners, this panel can feel overwhelming, but it breaks down into a few core concepts:

*   **Font:** The specific typeface family you are using (e.g., Inter, Roboto, Times New Roman). Figma provides access to Google Fonts by default, alongside any fonts installed locally on your computer.
*   **Weight:** The thickness of the letters. Common weights include Regular, Medium, Bold, and Semi-Bold. Heavier weights naturally draw the eye, making them perfect for headings.
*   **Size:** Measured in pixels (**px**), this determines how large the text appears on your screen. 
*   **Line Height:** The vertical space between lines of text. If your text wraps to multiple lines, increasing the line height prevents the lines from crashing into each other, improving readability. It is often set as a percentage (e.g., 140%) or a pixel value.
*   **Letter Spacing:** The horizontal space between individual characters. Increasing this slightly can make uppercase labels easier to read.
*   **Text Alignment:** Determines how the text sits within its bounding box (Left, Center, Right, or Justified).

### Establishing Hierarchy with Text

To create a clear visual hierarchy, you need to establish distinct levels of text. A standard UI design typically uses three or four levels:

1.  **Headings:** Large font size, heavier weight (e.g., Bold). Used for page titles and major sections.
2.  **Subheadings:** Medium font size, medium weight. Used to break up content under a heading.
3.  **Body Text:** Smaller font size (usually 14px to 16px for screen readability), regular weight. Used for paragraphs and standard descriptions.
4.  **Labels/Captions:** Smallest font size (10px to 12px), sometimes uppercase with increased letter spacing. Used for timestamps, tags, or minor UI hints.

By varying these properties, you tell the user’s brain exactly what to read first, second, and third, without them having to think about it.

## Applying Color in Figma

Color breathes life into your shapes and text. In Figma, you apply color to objects using **Fills**. A Fill is exactly what it sounds like—a color, gradient, or image that fills the interior of a shape or the characters of a text layer.

When you select a shape (like a Rectangle drawn with the Shape Tools) or a Text Layer, look for the **Fill** section in the Right Sidebar. Clicking the color swatch next to the word "Fill" opens the color picker.

### Solid Fills

A **Solid Fill** applies a single, flat color to your element. When the color picker opens, you have several ways to define your color:

*   **The Color Canvas:** A large square where you can click and drag to choose a color and its brightness.
*   **The Hue Slider:** A vertical slider next to the canvas that lets you pick the base color (red, yellow, green, blue, etc.).
*   **Hex Code:** A text box where you can type a specific 6-character code (e.g., `#FFFFFF` for white, `#000000` for black). This is the most common way developers will ask you for a color.
*   **Opacity:** A percentage slider (often labeled with an eye icon) that determines how transparent the color is. An opacity of 100% is completely solid, while 50% means you can see through it to the layers beneath.

*Note on Accessibility:* When applying solid colors, always keep **Accessibility** in mind. If you put dark blue text on a black background, users will struggle to read it. Ensure there is a high contrast between your text color and your background fill color.

### Gradient Fills

While solid fills make up the bulk of a UI, **Gradients** allow you to transition smoothly between two or more colors. Gradients are excellent for creating subtle depth on buttons or eye-catching background shapes.

To apply a gradient, click the Fill swatch, then look at the top of the color picker. You will see four small icons: Solid, Linear, Radial, and Angular. 

*   **Linear Gradient:** Colors transition in a straight line. Once selected, a long bar with dots (called "stops") will appear on your Canvas. You can drag these stops to change the angle and length of the gradient. Click a stop to change its specific color.
*   **Radial Gradient:** Colors radiate outward from a central point in a circular shape.
*   **Angular Gradient:** Colors transition around a central point, like the dial of a clock.

To add more colors to your gradient, simply click anywhere on the gradient bar on your Canvas to add a new stop.

## Creating Reusable Styles

Imagine you are designing an app with 30 different screens. On each screen, you have a primary heading using the Inter font, Bold weight, at 32px. You also have a primary brand color used for 50 different buttons across those screens. 

Suddenly, your client asks you to change the brand color from blue to purple, and the heading font from Inter to Roboto. If you manually styled every single layer, you would have to hunt down 50 buttons and 30 headings to update them individually. 

Figma solves this problem with **Design Styles**. Design Styles allow you to save a specific set of typography or color properties, give them a name, and apply them across your entire file. If you update the master Style, every element using that Style updates instantly.

### Text Styles

A **Text Style** saves all the typography properties of a selected Text Layer. 

To create one:
1. Select a Text Layer that you have perfectly formatted (e.g., your main heading).
2. In the Right Sidebar, look at the Text section. You will see a square icon with four small dots (this is the Style picker). Click it.
3. Click the **+** icon in the top right corner of the menu that appears.
4. Give your Style a descriptive name (e.g., "Heading / Large") and click **Create Style**.

Now, whenever you need a large heading, you can select any Text Layer, click the Style picker, and select "Heading / Large" to apply all those formatting rules instantly.

### Color Styles

A **Color Style** (sometimes called a Paint Style) saves a specific solid or gradient fill. 

To create one:
1. Select a shape or text layer that has the fill color you want to save.
2. In the Fill section of the Right Sidebar, click the Style picker (the square with four dots, located next to the color swatch).
3. Click the **+** icon.
4. Name your style based on its function rather than its appearance. For example, instead of naming it "Blue", name it "Background / Primary" or "Button / Brand Color". This way, if you change it to green later, the name still makes sense.

## Managing and Applying Styles

Once you start creating Styles, you need a way to keep them organized and apply them efficiently.

### The Assets Panel

All of your saved Text Styles and Color Styles live in the **Assets Panel**, located in the Left Sidebar next to the Layers tab. Click on the "Assets" tab to see a list of everything you have created.

From here, you can:
*   **Apply a Style:** Click and drag a Color Style directly onto a shape on your Canvas. 
*   **Search:** If you have dozens of styles, use the search bar at the top of the panel to find the exact one you need.
*   **Organize:** You can group styles by using a forward slash (`/`) when naming them. For example, creating a style named `Text/Body/Small` will automatically group it under "Text", then "Body", keeping your assets panel neat and tidy.

### Updating Styles

The true power of Styles lies in their ability to update globally. If you decide your "Button / Brand Color" needs to be a darker shade:

1. Select any shape that currently has that Color Style applied.
2. In the Fill section, click the color swatch to open the picker. Notice there is a button that says **Edit Style**. Click it.
3. Change the color in the picker. 
4. Click **Save**. 

Instantly, every single layer in your file using that Color Style will update to the new color. The same process applies to Text Styles—select the styled text, adjust the font or size, click the Style picker, and hit **Edit Style** to push the changes everywhere.

## A Real-World Scenario: Styling a Profile Card

Let’s see how this works together. Imagine you are designing a user profile card inside a Frame. 

1.  **Background:** You draw a Rectangle using the Shape Tools (**R**) to cover the Frame. You create a Color Style called `Surface/Background`, set it to a soft light gray (`#F5F5F5`), and apply it.
2.  **Avatar:** You draw a circle using the Ellipse Tool (**O**). You fill it with a Linear Gradient transitioning from a soft purple to a bright pink.
3.  **Name:** You add a Text Layer for the user's name. You set the font to Roboto, weight to Bold, and size to 24px. You apply a Color Style called `Text/Primary` (solid dark gray, `#333333`). You then save these text properties as a Text Style named `Heading/Medium`.
4.  **Bio:** You add another Text Layer for a short biography. You set it to Roboto, Regular, 16px, with a Line Height of 140% for comfortable reading. You apply a Color Style called `Text/Secondary` (solid medium gray, `#777777`). You save this as `Text/Body`.

A week later, your team decides the dark gray text is too harsh against the light gray background. Instead of hunting down every text layer, you simply go to your Assets Panel, edit the `Text/Primary` Color Style to a softer dark slate color, and save. The user's name, along with every other primary heading across your entire design file, updates instantly. 

## Key Takeaways

*   **Typography builds hierarchy:** By varying font, weight, size, and line height, you guide the user’s eye from the most important information to the least.
*   **Fills apply color:** Use Solid Fills for flat colors and Gradient Fills to transition smoothly between two or more colors. 
*   **Design Styles are time-savers:** Saving Text Styles and Color Styles allows you to maintain consistency and update your entire design file instantly.
*   **Name Styles by function, not appearance:** Naming a style `Button/Primary` rather than `Blue` ensures your styles remain logically named even if the colors change later.
*   **Accessibility is paramount:** Always ensure there is sufficient contrast between your text layers and background fills so users can easily read your interface.

modules.title: Components and Variants
modules.content:
## The Blueprint of Interface Design

Imagine you are designing an e-commerce app. On the home screen, you have a "Add to Cart" button. On the product page, you have another "Add to Cart" button. In the shopping cart, the checkout flow has an "Add to Cart" button. If you design each of these buttons from scratch—drawing the rectangle, picking the exact blue from your color palette, setting the typography, and adjusting the corner radius—you are doing a lot of repetitive work. 

But the real problem arises when your client says, "Let's make all the primary buttons green instead of blue." If you drew 50 buttons individually across your file, you now have to find and recolor all 50 of them. If you miss one, your User Interface loses its consistency, which damages your visual hierarchy and confuses the User Experience.

Figma solves this problem with a feature called **Components**. A component is a master element that you can reuse across your design file as many times as you want. When you update the master element, every copy of it updates instantly. In this chapter, we will explore how to create these master elements, use them, and organize them into powerful variant sets.

## Main Components vs. Instances

Before we build anything, we need to define two crucial terms in Figma: **Main Components** and **Instances**. Understanding the relationship between these two is the foundation of scalable UI design.

- **Main Component:** This is the original, master blueprint of your design element. It defines the structure, color, typography, and size of the element. You usually keep your main components on a separate frame away from your actual design canvas, almost like a storage shelf.
- **Instance:** This is a copy of the main component that you place into your actual design. An instance is permanently linked to its main component. 

Think of a Main Component as a rubber stamp, and an Instance as the mark it leaves on paper. If you carve a new design into the rubber stamp (edit the Main Component), every mark you make from then on will have the new design. 

Here is the magic of instances: while they are linked to the main component, you can still override certain properties on an individual instance. If your main component is a blue button with the text "Submit," you can drag an instance onto your canvas and change the text to "Cancel" without breaking the link. If you later decide to change the corner radius of the main component from 4 pixels to 8 pixels, all your instances will update their corners to 8 pixels, but they will retain their individual text ("Submit", "Cancel", "Save", etc.).

## Creating Your First Component

Let’s build a basic button. By now, you are familiar with using the **Frame Tool (F)** and drawing shapes. We will combine a background shape with a text layer to create our button.

1. Select the **Frame Tool (F)** and draw a frame on your canvas. 
2. In the right sidebar, give it a fill color from your Color Palettes—let's use a solid blue.
3. Adjust the corner radius of the frame in the right sidebar to 8 to give it slightly rounded edges.
4. Select the **Text Tool (T)**, click inside the frame, and type "Log In". Set your typography styles as needed.
5. Click on the frame in the layers panel, hold `Shift`, and click the text layer so both are selected.

Now you have a basic button. To turn this into a reusable Main Component, select both elements and use the keyboard shortcut `Ctrl+Alt+K` (Windows) or `Cmd+Option+K` (Mac). Alternatively, you can click the **Create Component** icon at the top of the right sidebar (it looks like four small squares forming a larger square).

When you do this, a few things happen:
- Your layers panel will show a new purple icon next to the layer name, indicating it is now a Main Component.
- The component is grouped together perfectly. 

### Reusing the Component

Now that you have a Main Component, it’s time to reuse it. Select your new Main Component in the layers panel, copy it (`Ctrl+C` / `Cmd+C`), and paste it (`Ctrl+V` / `Cmd+V`). 

Look closely at the layers panel. The pasted copy will have a slightly different purple icon—it looks like a solid diamond. This indicates that this element is an **Instance**, not a Main Component. 

You can now drag this instance anywhere on your canvas. If you duplicate it ten times, you will have ten instances. To test the connection, select your original Main Component and change its fill color to red. Watch as all ten instances instantly turn red. You have just saved yourself hours of future editing time.

### Overriding Instances

As mentioned, instances are not completely locked. You can customize them locally without affecting the Main Component or other instances. These customizations are called **Overrides**.

Select one of your button instances. In the right sidebar, you will see a section called **Instance** and below it, **Overrides**. Here, you can change:
- **Text:** Change "Log In" to "Sign Up".
- **Color:** Change the fill from red to gray.
- **Visibility:** Hide the text layer entirely.

If you ever want an instance to revert back to the exact state of the Main Component, simply select the instance and click the **Reset overrides** button (two curved arrows forming a circle) in the right sidebar.

## Organizing Components

When you are designing a real app, you will quickly accumulate dozens of components: buttons, input fields, navigation bars, cards, icons, and more. If you leave them scattered across your canvas, your file will become a messy nightmare to navigate. Figma provides a few ways to keep things tidy.

### The Assets Panel

Look at the left side of your Figma window, where the Layers panel lives. Click on the **Assets** tab next to Layers. This panel acts as a library of all the components in your current file. 

If you expand the "Local components" dropdown, you will see a list of all your Main Components. From here, you can drag and drop components directly onto your canvas. This means you can move your Main Components to a completely different area of your canvas—perhaps a dedicated "Component Storage" frame—and access them purely through the Assets panel, keeping your workspace clean.

### Naming Conventions

Figma uses a forward slash (`/`) in layer names to create a folder-like structure in the Assets panel. If you name your component `Button / Primary`, Figma will create a folder called "Button" and place a component named "Primary" inside it. 

If you have multiple button states, you might name them:
- `Button / Primary / Default`
- `Button / Primary / Hover`
- `Button / Primary / Disabled`

This hierarchical naming keeps your Assets panel organized and makes it much easier to find exactly what you need.

## Introduction to Variants

As your design system grows, you might find yourself with five different versions of a button in your Assets panel: Primary, Secondary, Danger, Success, and Disabled. While naming conventions help organize them, clicking through a long list to find the right button state can be tedious.

This is where **Variants** come in. A variant set is a collection of similar components grouped together into a single, smart family. 

Instead of hunting through the Assets panel for a "Secondary Disabled" button, you can drag a single "Button" component onto your canvas and use a dropdown menu in the right sidebar to switch it to Secondary and Disabled. 

## Combining Components into a Variant Set

To create a variant set, you first need multiple Main Components that share a similar purpose. Let’s build a set of buttons.

### Step 1: Create the Individual Components
Let's assume you have already created three separate Main Components:
1. A blue button labeled "Submit"
2. A gray button labeled "Cancel"
3. A red button labeled "Delete"

Ensure they are all the same size and structure, just with different colors and text.

### Step 2: Combine Them
1. Select all three Main Components on your canvas.
2. In the right sidebar, click the **Combine as Variants** button (it looks like a square with a smaller square inside it). 
3. Alternatively, right-click one of the selected components and choose **Combine as Variants** from the menu.

Figma will instantly group these components together into a purple dashed frame. This frame is your **Variant Set**. If you look in the Assets panel, those three separate components have now been merged into a single component family.

### Step 3: Define Properties and Values
When you combine variants, Figma asks you to define the **Properties** and **Values** that differentiate them. Properties are the characteristics you change between variants. Values are the specific options for those properties.

Look at the right sidebar when your variant set is selected. By default, Figma might guess a property name, but you should rename it to something logical. 

1. Click on the default property name (often called "Variant") and rename it to `Type` or `Style`.
2. Select the first component (the blue button) inside the set. In the right sidebar, set its value for the `Type` property to `Primary`.
3. Select the gray button. Set its value to `Secondary`.
4. Select the red button. Set its value to `Danger`.

### Step 4: Using the Variant Set
Now, go to your Assets panel and drag an instance of your new Button family onto the canvas. With the instance selected, look at the right sidebar. Instead of seeing a static element, you will see a dropdown menu labeled `Type` with `Primary`, `Secondary`, and `Danger` as options. 

You can now simply select the dropdown and switch the button's style instantly. This drastically reduces the number of items in your Assets panel and makes swapping between states incredibly fast.

### Adding Multiple Properties

Variant sets become truly powerful when you add multiple properties. Imagine you want your Primary, Secondary, and Danger buttons to also have a `Disabled` state (where the button is faded out and non-interactive).

Instead of creating six separate components, you can add a second property to your existing set.

1. Select your Variant Set frame.
2. In the right sidebar, click the **Properties** section and select **Variant**. 
3. Name this new property `State`.
4. You will now need to add the missing combinations. Figma's variant grid makes this easy. You will see a grid layout where one axis is `Type` and the other is `State`.
5. If you are missing a "Primary + Disabled" combination, you can select an existing component, copy it, and paste it into the empty grid slot. 
6. Select the new component, set its `Type` to `Primary` and `State` to `Disabled`. Then, visually edit this component (reduce its opacity or change its color to gray) to represent a disabled state.

Now, when you drag an instance onto your canvas, you have two dropdowns: `Type` and `State`. You can mix and match them (e.g., Type: Danger, State: Disabled) to get exactly the button you need without cluttering your file.

## Best Practices for Components and Variants

As you begin incorporating these tools into your workflow, keep a few principles in mind to ensure your design file remains a help, rather than a hindrance.

**Keep your Main Components organized:** Create a specific Frame on your canvas dedicated to holding your Main Components. Name it something like "Component Library" or "Design System." Keep it far away from your actual Artboards so you don't accidentally design over it.

**Use Auto Layout (Coming Soon):** While we aren't covering it in this chapter, be aware that combining Components with Auto Layout (which makes elements resize automatically based on their content) is the gold standard for UI design. For now, design your components with fixed sizes, but know that dynamic resizing is on the horizon.

**Don't over-variant:** It is tempting to put every single button state, size, and color into one massive variant set. However, if a variant set has too many properties, it becomes difficult to manage. A good rule of thumb is to use variants for structural or state changes (e.g., Default vs. Hover, Small vs. Large), and use instance overrides for minor text changes (e.g., "Log In" vs. "Sign Up").

**Maintain accessibility:** When creating variant sets for different states (like a disabled button), ensure you are still following accessibility guidelines. A disabled button should still have enough contrast against its background to be legible, even if it is faded. 

By mastering Components and Variants, you transform your design process from drawing static pictures to building a functional, interconnected system. This system approach ensures that as your project scales, your design remains perfectly consistent, and your updates take seconds instead of hours.

## Key Takeaways

- A **Main Component** is the master blueprint; an **Instance** is a linked copy placed in your design.
- Editing a Main Component updates all connected instances, but you can customize individual instances using **Overrides**.
- Use the **Assets panel** and forward slashes (`/`) in layer names to keep your components organized and easy to find.
- **Variants** allow you to group similar components (like different button styles) into a single smart family, reducing clutter in your Assets panel.
- **Properties** and **Values** define how variants differ. You can use multiple properties (e.g., `Type` and `State`) to create a matrix of options that can be swapped via dropdown menus.
- Keep your Main Components on a separate frame away from your active design canvas to maintain a clean workspace.

modules.title: Auto Layout for Responsive Design
modules.content:
## The Problem with Moving Pixels by Hand

Imagine you’ve just finished designing a perfect **Button**. You spent time adjusting the **Typography**, balanced the **Color palettes**, and set the exact spacing so the text sits beautifully in the center. You show it to your team, and they say, "We love it, but can we see what it looks like in German?" 

You type in the German translation. Suddenly, your perfect button breaks. The text overflows the background shape, the padding is ruined, and the text is no longer centered. You manually resize the background, adjust the text box, and re-center everything. Then, they ask for a version with an icon. You repeat the whole manual process.

This is how early UI design worked. Designers pushed pixels around by hand, resizing background rectangles every time the content inside them changed. 

**Auto Layout** is Figma’s solution to this problem. It is a property you can add to a **Frame** that automatically controls the spacing, padding, and alignment of the objects inside it. Instead of telling Figma exactly where an object should sit on the X and Y axes, Auto Layout tells Figma *how objects should relate to each other*. 

When you apply Auto Layout, frames stop being fixed-size boxes and become flexible containers that grow, shrink, and adapt to their content automatically. This is the foundational skill for creating responsive designs—interfaces that look great whether they are viewed on a tiny mobile phone or a massive desktop monitor.

## The Core Ingredients: Direction, Spacing, and Padding

When you apply Auto Layout to a frame, Figma shifts from standard absolute positioning (where you drag things around freely) to relative positioning. The Auto Layout menu in the right sidebar gives you control over four main ingredients. 

### 1. Direction
Direction dictates how the elements inside the frame flow. There are three options:
*   **Horizontal:** Elements sit side-by-side, left to right. Think of a row of navigation links.
*   **Vertical:** Elements stack on top of each other, top to bottom. Think of a news feed.
*   **Wrap:** Elements flow in a line, but if they run out of room, they wrap to the next line. Think of a grid of tags on a blog post.

### 2. Spacing (Gap)
Spacing (often called "gap") is the empty space *between* the items inside your Auto Layout frame. If you have three icons in a horizontal Auto Layout frame with a gap of 10 pixels, Figma will automatically put 10px of space between icon one and two, and 10px between icon two and three. If you add a fourth icon later, it automatically gets its 10px gap.

### 3. Padding
While spacing controls the gaps between items, **Padding** controls the space between the items and the edge of the frame itself. 
*   **Top** and **Bottom** padding control the vertical breathing room.
*   **Left** and **Right** padding control the horizontal breathing room.

If you have a text label inside a frame and you want 20 pixels of space on all sides, you set the padding to 20. If the text gets longer, the frame automatically stretches to keep that 20-pixel boundary intact.

### 4. Alignment
Alignment determines where the items sit along the main axis and cross axis. 
*   In a **Horizontal** frame, the main axis is horizontal, and the cross axis is vertical.
*   In a **Vertical** frame, the main axis is vertical, and the cross axis is horizontal.

You can align items to the top, bottom, left, right, or center them. By default, Figma centers items in Auto Layout frames, but you can easily click the alignment icons in the properties panel to push items to the start or end of the frame.

## Building Your First Responsive Button

Let’s put the core ingredients together by building a dynamic button. Since you already know how to create text and shapes from previous chapters, we will focus on how Auto Layout binds them.

1.  Select the **Text Tool (T)** and type "Submit". Style it however you like using the typography properties.
2.  Select the text layer. Instead of manually drawing a Rectangle shape behind it (which you would then have to resize manually), simply press **Shift + A** on your keyboard. 
3.  Figma instantly wraps your text in a new Auto Layout frame. The background of this frame is transparent by default. 
4.  In the right sidebar, look at the Auto Layout section. Change the **Padding** to 16px horizontally and 10px vertically. 
5.  Click the swatch under the "Fill" section of the frame to give it a background color. 

You now have a button. If you double-click the text and change "Submit" to "Submit Application", the background frame automatically grows wider to accommodate the longer text, keeping the padding perfectly intact. 

### Adding an Icon to the Button
Buttons frequently need icons. Let's add one to the left of the text without breaking our layout.

1.  Drag an icon (a simple arrow shape works well) into the Auto Layout frame, dropping it to the left of the text.
2.  Because the frame is set to **Horizontal** direction, the icon and text snap side-by-side. 
3.  If the icon and text are touching, increase the **Spacing (gap)** to 8px. 

Now, your button is a fully dynamic component. The icon stays a fixed size, the text changes size based on the characters, and the background frame stretches to fit them both perfectly. If you turn this into a **Component** (as covered in Module 4), you have just created a master button that will adapt to any text or language you throw at it.

## Resizing Rules: Hug, Fill, and Fixed

To make interfaces truly responsive, you need to tell Figma how frames should behave when the screen size changes. Auto Layout introduces three resizing rules. You will find these options in the dropdown menus next to the Width and Height fields in the right sidebar.

### Fixed
**Fixed** is the simplest rule. It means the width or height is locked to a specific number of pixels (e.g., exactly 300px wide). The element will not change size, regardless of the content inside it or the screen size around it.

### Hug
**Hug** tells Figma to shrink the frame down to exactly fit the content inside it, plus whatever padding you have set. When you created the button earlier, the width was set to "Hug". It hugged the text and icon tightly. Hug is perfect for buttons, tags, and small text boxes.

### Fill
**Fill** is the opposite of Hug. It tells the frame to expand to fill all available space inside its parent frame. If you place a frame inside a larger container and set its width to "Fill", it will stretch edge-to-edge to match the width of that container. Fill is essential for creating full-width banners, responsive cards, and grid layouts.

**The Golden Rule of Responsive Design:** You generally want parent frames to be "Fill" so they take up the screen, and child elements inside them to be "Hug" or "Fixed" so they maintain their proper sizes. 

## Designing Flexible Cards

Cards are a staple of modern User Interface design. They hold an image, a title, a short description, and maybe a button. Let’s use Auto Layout to build a card that adapts to different amounts of text.

1.  Draw a **Frame** (F). Leave it without Auto Layout for a moment; this will be our outer card container. Give it a white background, rounded corners, and a subtle drop shadow.
2.  Inside this frame, place an image at the top. Set the image frame's width to **Fill** and height to **Fixed** (e.g., 200px). 
3.  Below the image, add a text layer for the Title. Select the text and press **Shift + A** to add Auto Layout. Set the direction to **Vertical**. 
4.  Add a text layer for the Description and drop it inside the new Auto Layout frame, below the Title. Set the spacing between them to 8px. 
5.  Select this Auto Layout frame (the one holding your text) and set its width to **Fill**. Now it will stretch to be as wide as the outer card container. Set its padding to 16px on all sides.

Now, if you change the width of the outer card frame, the image fills the width, and the text frame fills the width. If you type a longer description, the text frame grows taller vertically, and the outer card frame automatically grows to accommodate it. No manual resizing required.

## Nesting Auto Layout Frames

The true power of Auto Layout reveals itself when you start nesting frames inside of frames. Just like Russian nesting dolls, an Auto Layout frame can be placed inside another Auto Layout frame. 

Consider a standard website **Navigation Bar**. It usually has a logo on the left, a few links in the center, and a profile icon on the right. 

1.  Create a main frame for the navigation bar. Set it to **Horizontal** direction. Set its width to **Fill** (so it stretches across the top of the screen) and height to **Fixed** (e.g., 64px). Set padding to 16px left and right.
2.  Put your Logo inside this frame.
3.  Create a *second* frame for the navigation links. Set it to **Horizontal**, with a gap of 24px. Put your "Home", "About", and "Contact" text links inside it.
4.  Put your Profile Icon inside the main navigation frame.

Right now, the logo, links, and icon are all bunched up on the left side of the main frame. How do we push the links to the center and the icon to the right? 

### Space Between
In the main navigation frame's Auto Layout settings, look for the alignment options. Beside the alignment boxes, there is a specific setting called **Space Between** (it looks like three dots spreading out). 

When you click **Space Between**, Figma takes any extra, empty space in the frame and distributes it evenly between the items. 

By applying Space Between to your navigation bar, the logo stays on the left, the links jump to the center, and the profile icon is pushed to the far right. If you resize the browser window (or the frame), the space between them expands and contracts fluidly. 

## Aligning Complex Elements

Sometimes, you have items in an Auto Layout frame that need different alignments. For example, you might have a row in a settings menu where the text label is aligned to the left, but a toggle switch is aligned to the right.

If you just drop a label and a toggle into a horizontal Auto Layout frame with "Space Between", it works perfectly. But what if you have a label, a secondary description below it, and *then* a toggle switch on the right?

1.  Create a horizontal Auto Layout frame. 
2.  Group the Title and Description text into their own vertical Auto Layout frame. 
3.  Place this vertical text frame and the Toggle switch into the main horizontal frame.
4.  Apply **Space Between**. 

The vertical text frame acts as a single unit. Figma pushes this unit to the left and the toggle to the right. By nesting a vertical frame inside a horizontal frame, you create complex, dynamic structures that still respond perfectly to screen width changes.

## Minimum and Maximum Widths

Responsive design isn't just about stretching infinitely. If a paragraph of text stretches across a 4K monitor, it becomes a single line that is 3000 pixels long. That is terrible for **Accessibility** and readability. 

Auto Layout allows you to set constraints on how much a frame can grow or shrink using **Minimum** and **Maximum** width/height values.

*   **Min Width:** Forces a frame to be at least this wide, even if the content inside it is smaller. This is useful for buttons, ensuring they never shrink so small they become unclickable.
*   **Max Width:** Caps the growth of a frame. If you set a text frame's width to "Fill" but give it a Max Width of 800px, it will stretch to fill the screen on a mobile device, but once the screen gets wider than 800px, the text frame will stop growing and remain centered.

## Practical Scenario: A Mobile App Feed

Let’s look at a realistic scenario to tie this all together. You are designing a social media feed for a mobile app. 

Each post contains:
1. A header (Profile picture, Username, and a timestamp).
2. An image.
3. Action buttons (Like, Comment, Share).
4. A caption.

Using standard frames, if one user writes a two-line caption and another writes a ten-line caption, you would have to manually resize the background of every single post. 

With Auto Layout, you build it once:
*   Create a main vertical Auto Layout frame for the Post Card. Set width to **Fill**.
*   The Header is a horizontal Auto Layout frame (Profile Pic + a vertical frame for Username and Timestamp). Set width to **Fill**.
*   The Image is a frame set to **Fill** width, with a fixed height.
*   The Actions are a horizontal Auto Layout frame with a gap of 16px. Set alignment to the left.
*   The Caption is a text layer set to **Fill** width.

Because everything is nested and set to fill the parent container, this post will automatically adjust its height based on the length of the caption. The background card will stretch seamlessly. If you duplicate this post 50 times to fill out your prototype, every single one will size itself perfectly based on the text inside it.

## Troubleshooting Common Auto Layout Issues

Auto Layout fundamentally changes how Figma behaves, and beginners often run into a few specific roadblocks.

**"My frame won't grow horizontally."**
Check your resizing rules. If your parent frame is set to "Hug" width, it will only be as wide as its contents. Change the parent frame's width to "Fill" so it knows it is allowed to expand to match the screen.

**"I can't drag items freely inside an Auto Layout frame."**
This is intentional. Auto Layout overrides absolute positioning. You cannot drag items up, down, left, or right to nudge them into place. Instead, you must change their position in the layers panel (dragging them above or below other layers) or adjust the padding, spacing, and alignment settings in the right sidebar.

**"My background disappeared."**
When you press Shift + A, Figma creates a frame with Auto Layout. Frames do not have a background fill by default. Make sure you select the frame itself (not the text inside it) and add a Fill color from the right sidebar.

**"My nested frame isn't filling the width."**
If you put a frame inside another frame and set the child to "Fill" but nothing happens, check the parent frame's width. If the parent is set to "Hug", it is shrinking to fit the child. The parent must be set to "Fill" or "Fixed" so the child has a defined boundary to fill.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Auto Layout replaces manual resizing:** It turns fixed frames into dynamic containers that automatically adjust spacing, padding, and alignment based on the content inside them.
*   **Four core controls:** Direction (Horizontal, Vertical, Wrap), Spacing (gap between items), Padding (space between items and the frame edge), and Alignment (where items sit on the axes).
*   **Resizing rules dictate behavior:** **Fixed** locks dimensions, **Hug** shrinks the frame to fit content, and **Fill** expands the frame to take up available space in its parent container.
*   **Nesting creates complex layouts:** Placing Auto Layout frames inside other Auto Layout frames allows you to build intricate components like navigation bars and app feeds that adapt fluidly.
*   **Space Between distributes empty room:** This setting automatically pushes elements to opposite ends of a frame, perfect for headers and navigation menus.
*   **Min and Max widths maintain readability:** Use these constraints to prevent text from stretching too wide on large screens or buttons from shrinking too small on mobile devices.

modules.title: Prototyping and Interactive Design
modules.content:
Imagine you are handed a beautifully bound book, but every single page is completely blank. You can feel the texture of the paper, admire the gold lettering on the cover, and appreciate the quality of the binding. Yet, without words on the page, the book serves no real purpose. You cannot read the story. 

Up to this point in your Figma journey, the screens you have designed are like that blank book. You have learned how to build sturdy **Frames**, arrange content beautifully using **Auto Layout**, style text with thoughtful **Typography**, and create reusable **Buttons and icons** using **Components and Variants**. Your User Interface (UI) looks fantastic. But right now, it is completely static. 

To understand how your product will actually feel to a user, you need to turn those isolated screens into a living, breathing experience. You need to simulate the journey a user takes when they tap a button, fill out a form, or navigate from a login screen to their home dashboard. 

This is where prototyping comes in. By the end of this chapter, you will bridge the gap between a static picture and a functional product, allowing users to click through your design as if it were a fully coded app.

## The Basics of Prototyping in Figma

In the world of UI/UX design, a **prototype** is an interactive simulation of your final product. It is not the final coded app or website, but it behaves like one. Prototyping allows you to conduct **User Experience** testing, demonstrate functionality to developers, and catch usability flaws before a single line of code is written.

In Figma, prototyping is built directly into the design file. You do not need to export your screens to a separate application to make them interactive. Everything happens on the same **Canvas** you have been using.

### Switching to the Prototype Panel

Take a look at the top right corner of your Figma window. You are likely very familiar with the **Design** tab, where you adjust colors, spacing, and Auto Layout properties. Right next to it is the **Prototype** tab. 

Clicking this tab changes the right-hand sidebar. The properties for fills, strokes, and typography disappear, replaced by tools designed to control movement, timing, and interaction. 

When you are in the Prototype panel, you will notice a small circular icon appear on the right edge of any element you select on your canvas. This is the **Connection Node**. This tiny circle is the key to all interactive design in Figma. It is how you tell your screens to talk to each other.

## Creating Navigation Connections

A **navigation connection** (often called an interaction or flow) is the link between two elements. The most basic prototype you can build involves clicking a button on one screen and having it take you to a completely different screen.

To create this, you need two things: a **trigger** (what the user does) and a **destination** (where the user goes). 

### Drawing Your First Connection Line

Let us look at a concrete scenario. Imagine you are designing a food delivery app. You have two **Frames** on your canvas: a "Sign In" screen and a "Home" screen. You want users to tap the "Sign In" button and be taken to the Home screen.

1. Ensure you are in the Prototype panel.
2. Select the "Sign In" button component on your first frame. 
3. Hover your cursor over the **Connection Node** on the right edge of the button. Your cursor will change into a crosshair.
4. Click and drag your mouse away from the button. You will see a line extending from the node, with a small target circle at the end.
5. Drag that target circle over to your second frame (the "Home" screen) and release the mouse click. 

Figma will draw an arrow connecting the two frames. You have just created your first interactive link!

### Anatomy of an Interaction

When you draw that connection line, Figma automatically opens a small menu along the arrow. This menu contains the three core building blocks of every Figma interaction:

*   **Trigger:** The user action that starts the interaction. By default, Figma sets this to **On click**. This means the interaction will happen when a user clicks (or taps, on a mobile device) the button. Figma offers other triggers as well, such as *On hover* (when the mouse moves over an element) or *After delay* (which happens automatically after a set amount of time).
*   **Destination:** The frame the user is taken to. This will automatically populate with the name of the frame you dragged the line to.
*   **Animation:** How the user gets to the destination. Does the screen appear instantly? Does it slide in from the side? We will explore this in detail shortly.

### The Noodle: Managing Connection Lines

As you add more connections, your canvas can quickly become tangled with arrows crisscrossing every which way. Designers affectionately refer to these prototyping lines as "noodles." 

To keep your file organized, you can click and drag the middle of any connection line to bend it. This allows you to route your noodles around your frames so they don't obscure your UI. If you need to delete a connection, simply click on the arrow to select it and press the Backspace or Delete key.

### Using the "Back" Button

In real apps, users don't just move forward; they go back. If a user navigates from a Home screen to a Settings screen, they need a way to return. 

To create a back navigation, you simply draw another connection line. Select the "Back" button on your Settings screen, grab its connection node, and drag it to the Home screen. You are creating a two-way street of interactions.

## Adding Interactive Animations

If all your prototype does is instantly swap one screen for another when a button is clicked, it will feel rigid and robotic. Real apps have motion. Menus slide out from the side, modals pop up in the center of the screen, and elements gently fade in. 

Animation in prototyping is not about making things flashy; it is about providing spatial awareness. It tells the user where they are, where they came from, and what just happened. Figma provides several ways to handle these screen transitions.

### Understanding Easing

Before we look at specific animations, you need to understand **easing**. In the physical world, objects do not start and stop instantly. A train pulling into a station gradually slows down before coming to a halt. A dropped ball accelerates as it falls. 

**Easing** is the mathematical principle applied to digital motion to make it feel natural. If a screen moves from left to right at a perfectly constant speed, it feels artificial. Figma applies default easing settings to your animations to make them feel smoother.

*   **Ease In:** The animation starts slowly and speeds up.
*   **Ease Out:** The animation starts quickly and slows down at the end. (This is generally the most natural-feeling default for UI elements entering the screen).
*   **Ease In and Out:** The animation starts slowly, speeds up in the middle, and slows down at the end.

You do not need to be a physics expert to use easing. When setting up an animation, simply choosing "Smart Animate" or a standard transition will apply a sensible easing curve for you, though you can adjust it if you want the motion to feel snappier or more sluggish.

### Screen Transitions

When you connect two entirely separate frames together, you use a **screen transition**. Figma offers several built-in transition types. Let us look at the most common ones you will use as a beginner:

*   **Instant:** The screen changes immediately with zero motion. This is the default, but you will rarely want to use it, as it feels jarring.
